description: Phase 3 Beta PRDs for Lando v4 'full breadth': complete the provider matrix, ship the full canonical service catalog, wire file sync via Mutagen, land all §11 subsystems, ship the global app + scratch apps, finish the recipe surface, complete the Landofile schema, wire the renderer at the CLI boundary, finish the tooling hot path, ship plugin install + library API, expand executable guides, and ship the 5-platform CI matrix + 4.0.0-beta.N publish. Stories follow the 13-PRD breakdown in `spec/beta/prd-beta-{01..13}-*.md`; each story has a corresponding US-XXX section in its PRD with full TDD acceptance criteria. Source of truth: `spec/ROADMAP.md` Phase 3 and complete spec parts in `spec/{01..19}-*.md`.

userStories.title: provider-lando Windows VM lifecycle
userStories.description: As a Windows tester, I can run `lando setup` and have `@lando/provider-lando` create, start, stop, and tear down the managed Podman machine without leaving WSL2/Hyper-V state behind. (PRD-B-01 US-074)
acceptanceCriteria: Fake-client unit tests cover create / start / stop / upgrade / teardown transitions for Windows
`lando setup` reports actionable remediation when virtualization prerequisites (Hyper-V, WSL2, Virtual Machine Platform) are missing
Live test gated behind `LANDO_TEST_WINDOWS_PROVIDER_LANDO=1` and skipped by default

userStories.title: provider-lando Windows runtime-bundle download and checksum verification
userStories.description: As a Windows tester, the runtime-bundle download path matches §5.8.1 — same checksum/signature semantics as Linux + macOS — and stores the bundle under the per-user state directory. (PRD-B-01 US-075)
acceptanceCriteria: Bundle URL and SHA-256 are resolved through the same pinned manifest used by Linux + macOS
Checksum mismatch fails closed with a tagged `ProviderBundleChecksumError`; remediation cites §5.8.1
Re-runs are idempotent (existing valid bundle is reused)

userStories.title: provider-lando Windows capability surface in meta:doctor
userStories.description: As a Windows tester, `lando doctor` surfaces every §5.4 capability for provider-lando and explains any missing one with a Windows-specific remediation. (PRD-B-01 US-076)
acceptanceCriteria: `meta:doctor` reports `bindMountPerformance`, `sharedCrossAppNetwork` (false until PRD-04), socket/machine status, and bundle version for the Windows provider
Snapshot-tested JSON output for the Windows path; live block skipped without `LANDO_TEST_WINDOWS_PROVIDER_LANDO=1`

userStories.title: provider-docker Windows (Docker Desktop)
userStories.description: As a Windows tester with Docker Desktop, I can use `@lando/provider-docker` to apply, inspect, exec, log, and destroy plans through the Docker Engine HTTP API. (PRD-B-01 US-077)
acceptanceCriteria: Docker Desktop named-pipe / TCP discovery: `DOCKER_HOST`, `npipe://./pipe/docker_engine`, fall-back order documented and tested
Fake-client tests cover apply / inspect / exec / logs / destroy
Live test gated by `LANDO_TEST_WINDOWS_DOCKER_SOCKET` or `DOCKER_HOST` and skipped by default

userStories.title: ship @lando/provider-podman as an opt-in provider (Linux)
userStories.description: As a Linux user with a pre-existing rootless Podman install, I can opt into `@lando/provider-podman` and reuse the Podman API contract. (PRD-B-01 US-078)
acceptanceCriteria: Plugin published at `@lando/provider-podman` with a `RuntimeProvider` Live Layer; not auto-loaded — opt-in via Landofile or `--provider=podman`
Discovers a user-installed Podman socket (`$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/podman/podman.sock` or `DOCKER_HOST`-style override)
Selection rejects opt-in if `lando setup` recorded a conflict with `@lando/provider-lando`'s private socket; remediation describes the conflict

userStories.title: @lando/provider-podman on macOS + Windows via Podman Desktop
userStories.description: As a macOS/Windows user with Podman Desktop, I can drive `@lando/provider-podman` against the Podman Desktop machine. (PRD-B-01 US-079)
acceptanceCriteria: Discovery covers the Podman Desktop default machine names + sockets per platform
Fake-client tests cover apply / inspect / exec / logs / destroy and machine-not-running remediation
Live tests gated by `LANDO_TEST_PODMAN_DESKTOP_SOCKET` and skipped by default

userStories.title: shared provider contract suite covers all three providers
userStories.description: As a provider maintainer, I can run one contract suite (from `@lando/sdk/test`) and have it cover `TestRuntimeProvider`, `provider-lando`, `provider-docker`, and `provider-podman` on every supported platform. (PRD-B-01 US-080)
acceptanceCriteria: `@lando/sdk/test` exports a single contract-suite runner; the four provider packages each have a contract-test entry point

Suite covers lifecycle (apply / inspect / exec / logs / destroy), capability matrix, error contract (`ProviderCapabilityError`, `NoProviderInstalledError`, redacted-detail rules from §5.7), and bundle/setup invariants
Suite is matrix-driven over platform × provider; cells without a supported declaration are skipped with a reason

userStories.title: provider negative-path coverage (partial-failure cleanup, abort signal)
userStories.description: As an operator, failed apply/destroy operations honor the abort signal and converge to a documented partial-state. (PRD-B-01 US-081)
acceptanceCriteria: Fake-provider tests cover partial apply failure after network creation and after one service start; cleanup preserves app-scoped volumes unless `destroy({ volumes: true })` is requested
All three providers honor the AbortSignal forwarded from the CLI; pending operations stop before bring-up completes
Errors include `providerId`, operation name, redacted details, and original cause per §5.7

userStories.title: provider selection precedence and conflict diagnostics
userStories.description: As a user with multiple providers installed, selection follows `flag > Landofile > env > config > capability-based default` and conflicts are diagnosed clearly. (PRD-B-01 US-082)
acceptanceCriteria: Precedence resolver tested for every pair of (`--provider`, Landofile `provider:`, `LANDO_PROVIDER`, `~/.lando/config.yml`, capability default)
`meta:doctor` reports the resolved provider and the inputs that led to it
Conflict between `provider-lando` and `provider-podman` (both targeting Podman API) is reported with remediation (`lando setup --provider=…`)

userStories.title: go service type (compiled and module-based projects)
userStories.description: As a user with a Go app, I can declare `type: go` (`go:1.22`, `go:1.23`) and `lando start` brings up a service with the Go toolchain available for `go run` / `go build` / `go mod`. (PRD-B-02 US-083)
acceptanceCriteria: `go` `ServiceType` shipped from `@lando/service-lando` with image tags pinned per §6.12.1
Scenario test starts a minimal Go HTTP server, verifies endpoint, and runs `lando go version` through tooling
Service contract test passes (capabilities, healthcheck schema, endpoints)

userStories.title: mongodb service type
userStories.description: As a user, I can declare `type: mongodb` (e.g. `mongodb:7`) and connect via `mongodb://lando@…` from sibling services. (PRD-B-02 US-084)
acceptanceCriteria: Default credential generation per §6.6 redaction rules
Scenario test brings up Mongo, runs `lando mongosh --eval "db.runCommand('ping')"` through tooling, asserts success

userStories.title: memcached service type
userStories.description: As a user, I can declare `type: memcached` and connect via `memcached://…` from sibling services. (PRD-B-02 US-085)
acceptanceCriteria: Scenario test sets and gets a key via `lando memcached-tool` (or equivalent shipped tooling alias)

userStories.title: valkey service type
userStories.description: As a user, I can declare `type: valkey` and use it as a drop-in replacement for `redis` (Valkey is the OSS Redis fork). (PRD-B-02 US-086)
acceptanceCriteria: `valkey` `ServiceType` shares schema with `redis`; endpoint emission emits both `valkey://` (primary) and `redis://` (alias for client-compat) per §6.12.1
Scenario test ping/set/get; tooling alias `lando valkey-cli` works
Service description documents the dual-scheme emission and recommends `valkey://` for new apps that want protocol awareness; `redis://` alias keeps existing client libraries working

userStories.title: solr service type
userStories.description: As a user, I can declare `type: solr` (e.g. `solr:9`) and post documents / run admin queries through tooling. (PRD-B-02 US-087)
acceptanceCriteria: `solr` `ServiceType` with HTTP healthcheck on the admin ping endpoint
Default core/collection creation is opt-in via service-level `cores:` config (deferred to RC if expressions-language ergonomics conflict — PRD-08)
Scenario test creates a default core and runs `lando solr-admin status`

userStories.title: elasticsearch service type
userStories.description: As a user, I can declare `type: elasticsearch` (Elastic License v2 image) and run cluster-health / index operations through tooling. (PRD-B-02 US-088)
acceptanceCriteria: Defaults to single-node, security-disabled local dev config; service description warns this is not production-suitable
Scenario test starts the service, asserts `green` or `yellow` cluster health, runs `lando es-cli` (or equivalent tooling alias) against `/_cat/indices`

userStories.title: opensearch service type
userStories.description: As a user, I can declare `type: opensearch` (Apache 2.0 image) and use the same query surface as `elasticsearch` against an Apache-licensed fork. (PRD-B-02 US-089)
acceptanceCriteria: Service description notes the licensing distinction vs Elasticsearch
Scenario test mirrors US-088 with the OpenSearch endpoint

userStories.title: meilisearch service type
userStories.description: As a user, I can declare `type: meilisearch` and use the Meilisearch HTTP API through tooling. (PRD-B-02 US-090)
acceptanceCriteria: Default master key generation per §6.6 redaction rules
Scenario test creates an index, posts a document, queries it via `lando meili` tooling alias

userStories.title: framework-preset coverage extends to Go (if §6.12.1 grows)
userStories.description: As maintainers, we keep the Beta cut of framework presets aligned with §6.12.1 — Beta does not add Drupal/Laravel/Symfony beyond Alpha but documents the Go framework slot as currently empty. (PRD-B-02 US-091)
acceptanceCriteria: `go` `ServiceType` declares `framework: "none"` only; future entries (Echo, Fiber, Gin, Chi, etc.) deferred to post-GA with §6.12.1 cross-reference
Documentation table in `@lando/service-lando` README lists supported `framework:` values per language; Beta adds the `go` row

userStories.title: service contract suite covers every new type
userStories.description: As a service-type maintainer, every new Beta service passes the `@lando/sdk/test` service-contract suite. (PRD-B-02 US-092)
acceptanceCriteria: Contract-suite runner is matrix-driven over service type × provider × platform
Each new service type from US-083..090 has a contract-test entry; capabilities, endpoint emission, healthcheck schema, default-credential redaction, and `LANDO_*` env contract are exercised

userStories.title: static service type
userStories.description: As a user, I can declare `type: static` (built-in nginx-backed) to serve a built artifact directory without writing a separate web-server service. (PRD-B-02 US-093)
acceptanceCriteria: `static` `ServiceType` accepts `root:` (path within the service) and emits an HTTP endpoint
No special framework integration; behaves like a minimal nginx serving `root:`
Scenario test mounts a `dist/` directory, starts the service, fetches a known file

userStories.title: raw Compose passthrough service
userStories.description: As a power user, I can declare `type: compose` with a `compose:` block that the planner passes through verbatim to the provider, while still respecting the Compose allowlist from §7.4. (PRD-B-02 US-094)
acceptanceCriteria: `compose` `ServiceType` accepts only keys in the documented Compose subset; rejected keys produce a tagged remediation per the cross-cutting Compose allowlist
The passthrough still emits `LANDO_*` env vars, app-root bind mount (unless explicitly opted out per service), and per-app network membership
Scenario test exercises a representative passthrough (e.g. a third-party image not in the canonical catalog) and asserts the service starts and gets a default endpoint

userStories.title: FileSyncEngine contract published in @lando/sdk
userStories.description: As a future file-sync plugin author, I can implement against a stable `FileSyncEngine` Effect Service tag with a documented contract. (PRD-B-03 US-095)
acceptanceCriteria: `FileSyncEngine` service tag, contract, and tagged errors (`FileSyncStartError`, `FileSyncDriftError`, `FileSyncStopError`) exported from `@lando/sdk`
Contract suite stub in `@lando/sdk/test` covers start / pause / resume / stop / status, error semantics, and idempotency
Schema round-trips through the §13.2 snapshot gate

userStories.title: @lando/file-sync-mutagen plugin Live Layer
userStories.description: As a user on a slow-bind-mount provider, the bundled `@lando/file-sync-mutagen` plugin provides a `FileSyncEngine` Live Layer that drives the Mutagen host CLI against the planned app-root mount. (PRD-B-03 US-096)
acceptanceCriteria: Plugin `Layer` declared in `plugins/file-sync-mutagen/src/index.ts`, registered in `BUNDLED_PLUGINS`, contributed to bundled.ts codegen
Fake-client unit tests cover create / pause / resume / terminate session and status polling
Mutagen sessions named by `${appId}-${serviceId}-${mountId}`; session names are deterministic

userStories.title: Mutagen host CLI + agent download via lando setup
userStories.description: As a user, `lando setup` downloads the platform-appropriate Mutagen host CLI plus the per-target agent and stores them under the user data root. (PRD-B-03 US-097)
acceptanceCriteria: Download manifest pinned per §17.9 (host + agent variants); SHA-256 verified per §5.8.1 semantics
Failure modes (network, checksum mismatch) reported with tagged remediation
Re-runs are idempotent (existing valid binaries reused)
`lando doctor` reports the resolved Mutagen version per platform

userStories.title: planner auto-selects file sync for slow-bind-mount providers
userStories.description: As a user, I do not configure file sync — the planner auto-selects `@lando/file-sync-mutagen` when the selected provider declares `bindMountPerformance: "slow"`, and skips file sync otherwise. (PRD-B-03 US-098)
acceptanceCriteria: `AppPlanner` consults `ProviderCapabilities.bindMountPerformance`; produces a `FileSyncPlan` per slow service mount
`BuildOrchestrator` starts file sync after the provider applies and before `post-app-start` lifecycle event publishes
Scenario test on Linux x64 (with a fake "slow" provider) verifies that `FileSyncPlan` is included and that `lando stop` terminates the sync session

userStories.title: excludes patterns (volume-shadow + Mutagen ignores)
userStories.description: As a user, `excludes:` patterns from §6 are honored by file sync — node_modules, vendor, etc. — using both the existing volume-shadow approach (Alpha) and Mutagen ignore rules. (PRD-B-03 US-099)
acceptanceCriteria: `excludes:` patterns translated into Mutagen ignore rules in addition to the volume-shadow strategy
Defaults include `node_modules`, `vendor`, `.git`, `tmp`, and any framework-aware presets contributed by service types
Scenario test confirms excluded paths are not synced (new file in excluded dir does not propagate to the container)

userStories.title: file-sync engine contract suite passes
userStories.description: As a maintainer, the `FileSyncEngine` contract suite is green against `@lando/file-sync-mutagen` on macOS arm64, macOS x64, and Windows x64. (PRD-B-03 US-100)
acceptanceCriteria: Contract-suite runner from US-095 wired against the Mutagen Live Layer
Live tests gated behind `LANDO_TEST_FILE_SYNC_LIVE=1` and skipped without a real provider socket + Mutagen binary
Nightly CI matrix exercises the suite on all slow-provider platforms

userStories.title: ProxyService contract + default Live Layer wired through global app
userStories.description: As a user, an HTTPS-aware reverse proxy automatically routes `https://<app>.lndo.site` to the right service without manual Traefik config. (PRD-B-04 US-101)
acceptanceCriteria: `ProxyService` Effect Service tag, contract, and tagged errors published in `@lando/sdk`
Default Live Layer routes through Traefik running inside the global app (per §20.10.1); contributed via `globalServices:` (PRD-05)
Per-app config (hostname rules, port mappings) is derived from service endpoints — no `proxy:` Landofile block required for the default case
Scenario test starts two apps with overlapping default ports; both resolve via Traefik with no port conflict

userStories.title: CertificateAuthority via @lando/ca-mkcert
userStories.description: As a user, local HTTPS works out of the box — Traefik gets a trusted certificate for `*.lndo.site` (or the configured base domain) without me running `mkcert -install`. (PRD-B-04 US-102)
acceptanceCriteria: `@lando/ca-mkcert` bundled plugin downloads mkcert via `lando setup`, runs `mkcert -install` once (with consent), and issues per-app wildcard certs
User can opt out via `lando setup --ca=none` (no trust install, HTTP only)
Scenario test asserts the issued cert chain validates against the locally-installed root

userStories.title: SshService sidecar (default)
userStories.description: As a user, services that need an SSH agent (e.g. Composer pulling from private git) get one via a sidecar container — no `ssh-agent` lifecycle on the host. (PRD-B-04 US-103)
acceptanceCriteria: `SshService` contract published in `@lando/sdk`; default Live Layer is the sidecar implementation per §10.4
Sidecar forwards a Unix-socket SSH agent into the app network; opt-in per service via `sshAgent: true`
`lando ssh <service>` works for the sidecar path (provider-exec TTY still wraps the actual login)
Scenario test runs `lando composer install` against a fake private package server with SSH-agent auth and asserts success

userStories.title: HealthcheckService against provider-exec semantics
userStories.description: As a user, services declare healthchecks (`type: tcp|http|cmd`) and Lando waits for them before publishing `post-app-start`. (PRD-B-04 US-104)
acceptanceCriteria: Live Layer drives provider-exec for `cmd:` healthchecks and TCP/HTTP probes from inside the per-app network
Healthcheck failure within timeout → `HealthcheckTimeoutError` with the failing service, probe, and last status

userStories.title: ScannerService for endpoint discovery and port-collision detection
userStories.description: As a user, `lando info` reports the actual reachable endpoints and `lando start` errors clearly when two apps want the same host port. (PRD-B-04 US-105)
acceptanceCriteria: `ScannerService` contract published; default Live Layer combines provider inspect output with proxy routing rules
Port-collision errors include all conflicting apps + service ids
`lando info --json` includes the scanner output verbatim

userStories.title: HostProxyService for lndo.site-style hostnames
userStories.description: As a user, `*.lndo.site` resolves to `127.0.0.1` (or the configured loopback) without me editing `/etc/hosts`. (PRD-B-04 US-106)
acceptanceCriteria: `HostProxyService` contract + default Live Layer (DNS sinkhole or `/etc/hosts` writer per platform)
Privileged operations gated behind sudo/UAC prompt at `lando setup` time; never inline during `lando start`
`lando setup --host-proxy=none` opt-out for users managing their own DNS

userStories.title: sharedCrossAppNetwork capability + provider-side wiring
userStories.description: As the global app, I can reach per-app services across networks without each user enabling host-only networking. (PRD-B-04 US-107)
acceptanceCriteria: `ProviderCapabilities.sharedCrossAppNetwork: true` declared for `provider-lando` and `provider-docker` on every platform where it works; `false` where it does not, with remediation
Planner refuses to start a global service requiring `sharedCrossAppNetwork` on a provider without the capability — actionable error
Scenario test exercises global Traefik routing to a per-app web service across the shared network

userStories.title: lando doctor reports every subsystem status
userStories.description: As an operator, `lando doctor` aggregates every subsystem's status (proxy reachable, CA installed, host proxy active, healthcheck engine ready, scanner cache age) and surfaces remediation per §10.9. (PRD-B-04 US-108)
acceptanceCriteria: Per-subsystem doctor checks return a `{ status, severity, context, solution }` record per §10.9
JSON renderer snapshot (`meta-doctor.subsystems.ndjson`) covers every subsystem and at least one failing-state remediation
Doctor never requires app bootstrap unless `--app` is passed

userStories.title: networking intent — per-app bridge + shared cross-app network
userStories.description: As the planner, I produce a `NetworkingPlan` per app that creates a per-app bridge network AND attaches the app to a shared network when a global service or another app needs to reach it. (PRD-B-04 US-109)
acceptanceCriteria: Provider apply creates / joins both networks idempotently; destroy removes per-app bridge but leaves the shared network (which is owned by the global app)
Scenario test verifies two apps + global Traefik all reach each other

userStories.title: subsystem failure-recovery + cleanup
userStories.description: As an operator, partial subsystem failures (proxy down, CA expired, host proxy unreachable) leave the apps in a documented partial state and `lando doctor` explains how to recover. (PRD-B-04 US-110)
acceptanceCriteria: Each subsystem's failure path produces a tagged error with `severity` + `solution` per §10.9
`meta:doctor --fix` (where safe) re-runs the subsystem's setup step; otherwise produces a `manual` remediation
Tests cover at least one degraded-subsystem scenario per subsystem

userStories.title: GlobalAppService core service + reserved id global
userStories.description: As the runtime, the `global` app id is reserved; any user attempt to declare it in a Landofile is rejected with `AppIdReservedError`. (PRD-B-05 US-111)
acceptanceCriteria: `GlobalAppService` Effect Service tag, contract, and Live Layer in `@lando/core`
`AppIdReservedError` tagged error published in `@lando/sdk`; emitted when a Landofile declares `name: global` or any id resolving to `global`
Schema round-trips through the §13.2 snapshot gate

userStories.title: <userDataRoot>/global/.lando.yml materialization
userStories.description: As the runtime, the global app has a materialized Landofile under the per-user data root; `meta:global:install` writes it idempotently. (PRD-B-05 US-112)
acceptanceCriteria: `<userDataRoot>/global/.lando.yml` is generated by `meta:global:install`; reruns are idempotent
The materialized file references bundled global services + any plugin-contributed `globalServices:` entries
User edits to the global Landofile are preserved across `meta:global:install` reruns; conflicts produce a tagged remediation

userStories.title: globalServices: plugin contribution surface
userStories.description: As a plugin author, I can contribute `globalServices:` from my plugin's `Layer` and have those services appear in the global app's Landofile. (PRD-B-05 US-113)
acceptanceCriteria: `globalServices:` plugin contribution shape published in `@lando/sdk`; resolver merges contributions deterministically (last-wins per spec)
Conflict between two plugin contributions on the same service id produces a tagged error at bootstrap
Contract suite verifies a fake plugin contributing a global service appears in the materialized global Landofile

userStories.title: bundled Traefik global service
userStories.description: As a user, Traefik runs inside the global app and provides the default proxy referenced by per-app services. (PRD-B-05 US-114)
acceptanceCriteria: `traefik` global service shipped from `@lando/service-lando` (or a dedicated `@lando/service-traefik` plugin — implementer's choice; spec §20.10 is silent on packaging)
Default config exposes the Traefik dashboard on `traefik.lndo.site` (or configured base domain)
Scenario test (with global app started) confirms a per-app service is reachable through Traefik

userStories.title: bundled Mailpit global service
userStories.description: As a user, Mailpit runs inside the global app and per-app services have `MAIL_HOST`/`MAIL_PORT` env vars pointing at it. (PRD-B-05 US-115)
acceptanceCriteria: `mailpit` global service shipped; SMTP and web UI exposed (`mailpit.lndo.site` by default)
Per-app `LANDO_*` env contract extends to include `LANDO_MAIL_HOST` + `LANDO_MAIL_PORT` (resolved against the shared cross-app network)
Scenario test sends mail from a per-app service and asserts it appears in Mailpit's UI/API

userStories.title: meta:global:* CLI namespace
userStories.description: As an operator, I can run `meta:global:install`, `meta:global:start`, `meta:global:stop`, `meta:global:status`, `meta:global:uninstall`, `meta:global:config`, and `meta:global:destroy`. (PRD-B-05 US-116)
acceptanceCriteria: All seven commands implemented; aliases per §8.2 (e.g. `lando global:install`)
`meta:global:destroy` is gated behind `--yes` confirmation per the cross-cutting destructive-confirmation rule
Compiled `$bunfs` parity for every command (or unified dispatch from PRD-09 covers it)

userStories.title: Global lifecycle event scope
userStories.description: As an event subscriber, I receive `pre-global-start`, `post-global-start`, `pre-global-stop`, `post-global-stop` events distinct from per-app lifecycle events. (PRD-B-05 US-117)
acceptanceCriteria: Event payloads published in `@lando/sdk` with schema; `scope: "global"` in the event payload distinguishes them from per-app `scope: "app"`
Plugin subscribing to `post-global-start` receives the event before any per-app service that declared `AppFeature.requires.globalServices` boots

userStories.title: auto-start via AppFeature.requires.globalServices
userStories.description: As a user, running `lando start` on an app that needs the proxy will auto-start the global app if it is not running — no manual `meta:global:start`. (PRD-B-05 US-118)
acceptanceCriteria: Auto-start respects the abort signal; if global app start fails, per-app start fails with a chained tagged error pointing at the global failure
`lando stop` does NOT auto-stop the global app — global stop is explicit only

userStories.title: global-app integration with lando doctor
userStories.description: As an operator, `lando doctor` reports global app status (installed, started, contributing-plugin manifest, proxy / mailpit reachability). (PRD-B-05 US-119)
acceptanceCriteria: Output covers materialized Landofile path, last `meta:global:install` timestamp, per-service status, and plugin contribution list

userStories.title: ScratchAppService + scratch bootstrap level
userStories.description: As the runtime, scratch apps boot through a dedicated `scratch` bootstrap level that loads the minimum subset needed (no app discovery, no plugin tooling). (PRD-B-06 US-120)
acceptanceCriteria: `ScratchAppService` Effect Service tag + contract published in `@lando/sdk`
`scratch` added to `BootstrapLevel` ranking between `commands` and `provider` (or per spec — implementer confirms with §3.2)
Live Layer composed in `core/src/runtime/layer.ts`; scratch bootstrap takes no Landofile from CWD

userStories.title: apps:scratch:* CLI namespace
userStories.description: As a user, I can run `apps:scratch:start`, `apps:scratch:stop`, `apps:scratch:destroy`, `apps:scratch:list`, `apps:scratch:info`, `apps:scratch:logs`, and `apps:scratch:gc`. (PRD-B-06 US-121)
acceptanceCriteria: All seven commands implemented and aliased per §8.2
`--detach` flag on `apps:scratch:start` returns the scratch app id immediately and runs lifetime in the background

userStories.title: fork mode (apps:scratch:start --fork=<app>)
userStories.description: As a user, I can fork an existing app into a scratch copy with the same services, mounts, and config. (PRD-B-06 US-122)
acceptanceCriteria: Fork mode resolves the source app via `AppRef`, deep-copies the plan, and creates a new scoped id (e.g. `<source>-scratch-<rand>`)
Source app is not modified; fork's volumes/mounts are separate
Scenario test forks a Node+Postgres app, asserts both source and fork run simultaneously

userStories.title: scratch mode (apps:scratch:start --recipe=<id>)
userStories.description: As a user, I can scratch-start from any recipe (canonical or remote, per PRD-07) and get an ephemeral app without scaffolding into the CWD. (PRD-B-06 US-123)
acceptanceCriteria: Scratch mode runs the recipe against a temp directory under the per-user data root (`<userDataRoot>/scratch/<id>/`)
All recipe prompts that take defaults can be auto-answered via `--option=key=value` flags; remaining prompts run interactively unless `--yes`

userStories.title: --isolate=full content copy
userStories.description: As a user, `--isolate=full` causes the scratch app to be a content copy (not a shared bind mount) of the source app's app root. (PRD-B-06 US-124)
acceptanceCriteria: `IsolateMode` schema in `@lando/sdk`: `"none" | "full"`. `--isolate=full` is opt-in; default is `"none"` (shared mount with source)
Content-copy implementation uses platform-appropriate fast copy (`cp -r --reflink=auto` on Linux where supported, falling back to byte copy)
Provider declares `copyOnWriteAppRoot: false` for Beta; reflink/clonefile/overlay paths are preserved as the post-4.0 substitution point

userStories.title: scope-bound lifetime + finalizer
userStories.description: As a user running `apps:scratch:start` without `--detach`, the scratch app is destroyed automatically when the command exits — even on Ctrl-C. (PRD-B-06 US-125)
acceptanceCriteria: Finalizer registered against the Effect scope of the command; `Ctrl-C` triggers destroy
`--detach` skips the finalizer; the scratch app survives until `apps:scratch:destroy` or `gc`
Tests cover both attached + detached lifetimes including signal-on-exit cleanup

userStories.title: scratch-app registry + provider-label orphan reap
userStories.description: As an operator, `apps:scratch:gc` lists and removes scratch apps whose registry entry no longer matches a live provider container set. (PRD-B-06 US-126)
acceptanceCriteria: GC pass cross-references registry × provider label set; orphans on either side are reported; `--prune` removes them

userStories.title: --mount-cwd + --share-global-storage flags
userStories.description: As a user, I can opt the scratch app into mounting my current working directory and/or sharing the global app's storage scope. (PRD-B-06 US-127)
acceptanceCriteria: `--mount-cwd[=<container-path>]` adds a mount of `$PWD` into the scratch app's primary service
`--share-global-storage` joins the scratch app to the shared cross-app network and exposes the global app's storage scope
Flags are mutually compatible; tests cover both individually + together

userStories.title: apps:scratch:list + apps:scratch:info
userStories.description: As an operator, I can inspect what scratch apps exist, when they were created, what mode they were started in, and their lifetime status. (PRD-B-06 US-128)
acceptanceCriteria: `apps:scratch:list` renders id / source / mode / created / status (`attached` | `detached` | `orphan`)
`apps:scratch:info <id>` shows the same data plus mount points, network membership, and per-service endpoints
JSON renderer output covered by a named snapshot fixture

userStories.title: git recipe source
userStories.description: As a user, I can run `lando init --source=git --url=…` and Lando clones the repo, locates `recipe.yml`, and proceeds with prompts. (PRD-B-07 US-129)
acceptanceCriteria: `git` source resolver clones to `<userDataRoot>/recipe-cache/git/<sha>/`; SHA-pinned per-Landofile lock
Subpath (`--path=<dir>`) resolves the recipe inside a monorepo
Failure modes (auth, missing recipe.yml, dirty subpath) produce tagged remediation

userStories.title: tarball recipe source
userStories.description: As a user, I can run `lando init --source=tarball --url=…` and Lando downloads, verifies, and extracts the tarball before resolving `recipe.yml`. (PRD-B-07 US-130)
acceptanceCriteria: SHA-256 verification required when `--checksum=<hash>` is passed; warn-only otherwise (with a per-init prompt unless `--yes`)
Tests cover successful extract, checksum mismatch, and missing `recipe.yml` at top-level / subpath

userStories.title: npm recipe source
userStories.description: As a user, I can run `lando init --source=npm --package=@lando/recipe-…` and Lando fetches the npm tarball, extracts it, and proceeds. (PRD-B-07 US-131)
acceptanceCriteria: Resolves npm package metadata via the registry, downloads the published tarball, and reuses the `tarball` extractor
Honors `@version` suffix; defaults to latest stable

userStories.title: registry recipe source
userStories.description: As a user, I can run `lando init --source=registry --id=drupal-10` and Lando resolves the recipe id through the registry surface (e.g. `https://registry.lando.dev/recipes/`). (PRD-B-07 US-132)
acceptanceCriteria: `registry` source resolver hits the configured registry URL (default `https://registry.lando.dev/recipes/`) and follows the resolution result to a `git`/`tarball` underlying source
Tests use a fake registry; live integration test gated by env var

userStories.title: dynamic choicesFrom: for prompts
userStories.description: As a recipe author, I can declare a `select` prompt whose choices come from running a canonical Lando command (e.g. `lando services:list --type=php`). (PRD-B-07 US-133)
acceptanceCriteria: `choicesFrom: { command: "<id>", args: […], parse: "json" | "lines" }` schema published in `@lando/sdk`
Command runs in a sandboxed bootstrap that does not require an app; output parsed per `parse:`
Failure (command exit ≠ 0 or unparseable output) falls back to a remediation prompt asking the user for manual choice

userStories.title: runs: allowlist + ctx.run
userStories.description: As a recipe author, I can declare a `runs:` allowlist (command ids the recipe may invoke during `postInit:`) and call `ctx.run('<id>', args)` from declarative recipes or `recipe.ts`. (PRD-B-07 US-134)
acceptanceCriteria: `runs:` allowlist parsed from `recipe.yml` / `recipe.ts`; runtime check rejects any `ctx.run('…')` not in the allowlist with a tagged error
Allowlisted commands run under the same bootstrap as the recipe's own command
Tests cover allowlisted + non-allowlisted call paths

userStories.title: fetchAllowlist: + ctx.fetch
userStories.description: As a recipe author, I can declare a `fetchAllowlist:` (URL globs) and call `ctx.fetch(url)` from a recipe — calls outside the allowlist fail with a tagged remediation. (PRD-B-07 US-135)
acceptanceCriteria: `ctx.fetch` is a thin wrapper over the platform `fetch`; redirect targets must also match the allowlist
Tests cover allowed, denied, and redirect-out-of-allowlist cases

userStories.title: programmatic recipe.ts
userStories.description: As a recipe author, I can write a recipe as a TypeScript module exporting a default `Recipe` object instead of `recipe.yml`. (PRD-B-07 US-136)
acceptanceCriteria: `Recipe` type exported from `@lando/sdk`; a recipe TypeScript file is compiled and loaded under the recipe bootstrap
Compilation uses Bun's native TS support; no `tsc` step required at recipe-load time
Tests cover a fixture recipe.ts with prompts, `runs:`, `fetchAllowlist:`, and `postInit:` declarations

userStories.title: remaining postInit: bun: verbs — script, add, create, run
userStories.description: As a recipe author, I can declare `postInit:` steps that run `bun script`, `bun add`, `bun create`, and `bun run` (in addition to Alpha's `bun install`). (PRD-B-07 US-137)
acceptanceCriteria: `script` runs an arbitrary bun script from the recipe's `scripts/` directory
`add` installs additional packages with optional `--dev` semantics
`create` runs `bun create <template>`; the template arg supports recipe-substituted variables

userStories.title: postInit: bun: { verb: x } (arbitrary bunx invocation)
userStories.description: As a recipe author, I can declare `postInit: { bun: { verb: x, args: ["...", "..."] } }` to run a one-off `bunx` command. (PRD-B-07 US-138)
acceptanceCriteria: `x` verb runs the equivalent of `bunx <args>`; output streams into the renderer's task tree
Allowlist enforcement: `x` is gated by `runs:` if a recipe declares one; recipes without `runs:` allow `x` by default (recipes opt into stricter sandboxing)

userStories.title: includes: + .lando.lock.yml
userStories.description: As a user, my Landofile can `includes:` other YAML/Landofile fragments and the resolved tree is pinned by `.lando.lock.yml`. (PRD-B-08 US-139)
acceptanceCriteria: `includes:` resolver supports relative paths, `git@…`/`https://…` URLs (reuse PRD-07 source surface where it makes sense), and npm packages
`.lando.lock.yml` records the resolved SHA + hash of every included fragment
Lock mismatch at load time produces `LandofileLockMismatchError` with remediation pointing at `lando app:includes:update`

userStories.title: app:includes:update
userStories.description: As a user, I can run `lando app:includes:update` to refresh every included fragment to its latest source-resolved version and rewrite `.lando.lock.yml`. (PRD-B-08 US-140)
acceptanceCriteria: Command re-resolves every `includes:` entry, recomputes SHAs, and writes the lockfile atomically
Compiled binary parity verified (or unified dispatch from PRD-09 covers it)

userStories.title: app:includes:verify
userStories.description: As CI, I can run `lando app:includes:verify` to confirm the lockfile matches the resolved tree without mutating anything. (PRD-B-08 US-141)
acceptanceCriteria: Read-only command; exits non-zero on lock mismatch with the same `LandofileLockMismatchError` schema as US-139
JSON renderer output covers the per-fragment status

userStories.title: configuration expressions language (§7.3.1) — parser
userStories.description: As a user, I can use `${{ … }}` expressions in Landofile values with pipes, filters, and the full operator set documented in §7.3.1. (PRD-B-08 US-142)
acceptanceCriteria: Expression parser implemented; the full `pipe | filter | filter` syntax + comparators + ternary supported per §7.3.1
Parse errors emit `LandofileExpressionParseError` with file + line + column
Tests cover the documented operator surface (one assert per documented filter)

userStories.title: expression evaluator + sandbox
userStories.description: As a user, evaluating expressions only sees a sandboxed context (config values, env, service metadata, secrets) — no arbitrary code or filesystem reads. (PRD-B-08 US-143)
acceptanceCriteria: Evaluator runs against a documented `ExpressionContext` published in `@lando/sdk`
No host FS / network / process access from inside an expression; attempt → `LandofileExpressionForbiddenError`
Tests cover happy path (config + env + secret) and forbidden access

userStories.title: bundled template engines — Handlebars + Mustache
userStories.description: As a recipe / Landofile author, I can declare `template: handlebars` or `template: mustache` and have the Landofile rendered through the bundled engine before parsing. (PRD-B-08 US-144)
acceptanceCriteria: `@lando/template-handlebars` and `@lando/template-mustache` shipped as bundled plugins; contributed to `bundled.ts` codegen
Template selection happens before YAML parse; template errors surface with template-source line numbers (not post-render line numbers)
Default template engine is none (raw YAML); opt-in per Landofile

userStories.title: env overrides (§7.6)
userStories.description: As a user, I can override any Landofile value via `LANDO_*` env vars with the precedence chain documented in §7.6. (PRD-B-08 US-145)
acceptanceCriteria: Generic `LANDO_CONFIG__path__to__value=…` overlay implemented (uses `__` as delimiter); replaces MVP's hard-coded four-var overlay
Precedence: command flag > env > `.lando.local.yml` > main Landofile > defaults
Tests cover every level of the precedence chain

userStories.title: SecretStore contract + env-backed default
userStories.description: As a user, I can reference `${{ secrets.MY_TOKEN }}` in Landofile and have it resolve via a pluggable `SecretStore`; the default reads from environment variables. (PRD-B-08 US-146)
acceptanceCriteria: Default Live Layer reads from `process.env`; missing secret → `SecretNotFoundError` with the secret id
Secrets are redacted in renderer / log output by default; secret values never appear in `lando info` JSON

userStories.title: config-translation framework (§7.4.1)
userStories.description: As a plugin author, I can register a `ConfigTranslator` that maps an external config format (e.g. a legacy `lando v3` `.lando.yml` style key) into the canonical v4 schema. (PRD-B-08 US-147)
acceptanceCriteria: `ConfigTranslator` Effect Service tag + contract published in `@lando/sdk`
Translator resolution is deterministic; multiple translators run in declared order; conflicts produce `ConfigTranslatorConflictError`
No translators are shipped in Beta core (the framework is the deliverable; concrete translators come post-GA)

userStories.title: app:config:translate command
userStories.description: As a user with a non-canonical config file (e.g. v3 Landofile), I can run `lando app:config:translate` to produce a v4 canonical file via registered translators. (PRD-B-08 US-148)
acceptanceCriteria: Command reads the input file, runs through registered translators, writes the canonical YAML next to it as `.lando.yml.canonical`
With zero translators registered, the command exits with a remediation pointing at the plugin install path

userStories.title: Landofile schema gate
userStories.description: As a maintainer, the full Landofile schema (`includes:`, `expressions`, `template:`, `secrets:`, `tooling:`, `services:`, …) round-trips through the §13.2 schema snapshot gate. (PRD-B-08 US-149)
acceptanceCriteria: JSON Schema exports for every new key; snapshot test asserts no fixture drift on `bun run codegen`

userStories.title: app:config:lint command
userStories.description: As an IDE / editor integration (or a developer who wants fast schema feedback without paying for the full doctor --app sweep), I can run `lando app:config:lint` and get a canonical-schema-only validation of the Landofile in the current app directory. (PRD-B-08 US-195)
acceptanceCriteria: `lando app:config:lint` validates the Landofile against the canonical JSON Schema (no translators, no doctor checks, no provider probes)
Exit code is 0 on clean, non-zero on schema violation; renderer emits structured violations (path, message, suggested fix) so editors can surface inline diagnostics
`--format=json` emits a stable JSON shape suitable for LSP-style consumers; `--format=text` (default) is human-readable
`lando doctor --app` invokes the same lint pass internally (single source of truth — no forked logic between standalone and doctor paths)
Command participates in the §13.1 layer coverage rules and the §13.4 renderer lint gate
userStories.notes: Added during paradigm review (PRD-B-08 §Open Questions item #21 resolution). Ships standalone for IDE/LSP integrations and is wired into doctor --app via shared lint implementation.

userStories.title: `bun run dev:guides` watch mode
userStories.description: As a developer using executable guides as the dev-time test driver, I can run `bun run dev:guides` (or single-guide mode with a path arg) and the codegen + typecheck + scenario test loop re-runs on every change. (PRD-B-12 US-196)
acceptanceCriteria: On any change: re-runs `build-guide-scenarios` for affected guides, re-runs `tsc --noEmit` on generated paths, re-runs only the affected guide's scenario tests
Single-guide mode: `bun run dev:guides docs/guides/<path>.mdx` constrains the loop to one guide for focused TDD
Source-mapped failures from PRD-A2-03 surface unchanged
Watch mode exits cleanly on SIGINT with no leftover `dist/` artifacts beyond a normal run
Tests cover fixture guide, script invocation, failing guide, single-guide path
userStories.notes: Added during paradigm review (PRD-B-12 §Guide-as-TDD discipline). Alpha2-04 explicitly deferred this to Beta+; Beta picks it up.

userStories.title: Beta feature coverage matrix (`docs/guides/INDEX.md`)
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I can see at a glance which Beta features are covered by guides via an authored `docs/guides/INDEX.md` matrix mapping each user-facing PRD feature to the guide(s) that exercise it. (PRD-B-12 US-197)
acceptanceCriteria: `docs/guides/INDEX.md` exists with a table: PRD | User Story | Feature | Guide Path | Status
At Beta cutover, every user-facing Beta PRD (01, 02, 03, 04, 05, 06, 07, 08, 10, 11) has at least one row in the matrix
`scripts/check-guide-coverage.ts` validates that every guide path in INDEX.md exists, every §Guide-Coverage-declared PRD has its rows, and every row points at a real file
`bun run check:guide-coverage` is wired into the per-PR CI gate between `lint:guides` and the test run
Tests cover green INDEX, missing guide file, missing PRD row, dangling INDEX entry
userStories.notes: Authored matrix (not generated) so it can carry status/notes columns that a frontmatter scrape can't express.

userStories.title: per-PRD `## Guide Coverage` section convention
userStories.description: As a maintainer, every user-facing Beta PRD declares a `## Guide Coverage` section listing guide paths owned by that PRD; internal/infra PRDs declare `None`. (PRD-B-12 US-198)
acceptanceCriteria: Every user-facing Beta PRD (01, 02, 03, 04, 05, 06, 07, 08, 10, 11) has a `## Guide Coverage` section with a table mapping user stories to guide paths
PRDs 09 and 13 explicitly state `## Guide Coverage: None — internal/infra PRD.`
`scripts/check-guide-coverage.ts` parses the section and asserts every path exists in `docs/guides/` and appears in `INDEX.md`
A user-facing PRD that lacks the section, or declares a non-existent path, fails the gate
Tests cover valid section, missing section, dangling path, internal-PRD 'None' declaration
userStories.notes: Decentralized declaration (per PRD) with centralized matrix (INDEX.md) — keeps each PRD self-describing while still giving one place to scan for gaps.

userStories.title: guide-drift CI gate
userStories.description: As CI, when a PR touches files under a CLI/source surface declared in any PRD's §Guide Coverage section, the PR MUST also touch one of the listed guide files or use the `Guide-Coverage-Skip:` escape hatch. (PRD-B-12 US-199)
acceptanceCriteria: `scripts/check-guide-drift.ts` reads §Guide Coverage sections to map CLI/source paths to guide paths
On a PR: diffs changed files against declared surfaces; touch-surface-without-guide fails with a remediation pointing at the §Guide Coverage section
Escape hatch: PR body line `Guide-Coverage-Skip: <reason ≥ 24 chars>` bypasses; reason recorded in CI log and PR check summary
Wired as **blocking** into per-PR CI gate after `check-guide-coverage`
Tests cover: touch-without-guide (fail), touch-both (pass), guide-only (pass), valid skip (pass + log), too-short skip (fail)
userStories.notes: Blocking from day one of Beta (no soft-warning ramp). Escape hatch (`Guide-Coverage-Skip:`) handles legitimate exceptions like refactors and dependency bumps.

userStories.title: task.detail streaming tail (4-line ring buffer)
userStories.description: As a user watching `lando start`, each running task shows the last 4 lines of its stdout/stderr beneath the task line. (PRD-B-09 US-150)
acceptanceCriteria: `task.detail` events publish a single line; the renderer keeps a 4-line ring buffer per task
Buffer wraps oldest-out at exactly 4 lines (configurable later, fixed for Beta)
Tests cover ring-buffer rotation, finished-task collapse, and CSI cursor handling

userStories.title: task.detail.expand + task.detail.collapse keybindings
userStories.description: As a user, I can press a key to expand a task's full detail and another to collapse it back to the 4-line tail. (PRD-B-09 US-151)
acceptanceCriteria: Expand / collapse keybindings registered (default per §8.9; user-configurable post-GA)
Expanding shows the whole stream tail (bounded by terminal scroll); collapsing restores the 4-line ring
Tests use a fake TTY to exercise the keybinding state machine

userStories.title: full first-paint contract per §8.9.1
userStories.description: As a user, the first paint after `lando start` matches §8.9.1 exactly — banner, task tree placeholder, and no flicker. (PRD-B-09 US-152)
acceptanceCriteria: `paint.banner` first-paint contract honored; no Effect / OCLIF / SDK / plugin imports happen before first paint (covered by the canary test added in MVP)
Renderer initializes the task tree skeleton before any work runs
Tests use a fake terminal recorder to assert byte-for-byte first-paint output

userStories.title: verbose renderer
userStories.description: As a debugging user, I can pass `--renderer=verbose` to get the same task tree as `lando` plus full message and event traces. (PRD-B-09 US-153)
acceptanceCriteria: Output is human-readable but extends `lando` with every published event payload
Tests cover the renderer-selection precedence (`flag > env > config > default`) with `verbose` as a valid choice

userStories.title: renderer message contract (message.info / warn / error)
userStories.description: As a developer, every user-facing message goes through the renderer's `message.info`/`message.warn`/`message.error` contract — never `console.log` directly. (PRD-B-09 US-154)
acceptanceCriteria: Renderer message API exposed via the `Renderer` Effect Service tag
All four renderers (`lando`, `json`, `plain`, `verbose`) implement the message contract
Tests cover each renderer's output for each severity level

userStories.title: CLI dispatch unification spike
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I run the spike per ROADMAP Phase 3: can `@oclif/core`'s `execute()` dispatch reliably inside `bun build --compile` against `oclif.manifest.json` + `core/src/cli/oclif/compiled-commands.ts`? (PRD-B-09 US-155)
acceptanceCriteria: Spike covers `app:start`, `meta:setup`, `meta:bun --version` passthrough, and one deferred command id
Spike asserts exit code, stderr remediation, and JSON-renderer parity against `runCompiledCli`
Spike documented in `spec/14-appendices.md` with the conclusion (option a or option b)

userStories.title: apply spike outcome — unify or harden dual dispatch
userStories.description: As a maintainer, the spike outcome from US-155 is applied: either delete `runCompiledCli` and drop §8.4.1's relaxed-import rule, or promote §8.4.1 to normative and add a compiled-binary parity test layer. (PRD-B-09 US-156)
acceptanceCriteria: If option (a): `runCompiledCli` removed from `core/src/cli/run.ts`; §8.4.1 folded into a historical note; `AGENTS.md` interim bullets for dual CLI dispatch + single source of truth + compiled CLI coverage all removed
If option (b): §8.4.1 parity rules become normative; new §13.1 parity test layer covers every canonical command id (MVP_COMMAND_IDS + the §17.1 stage-7 deferred-command set)
Either way: §14.2 row 'Compiled-binary CLI dispatch unification' closed; ROADMAP Phase 3 spike entry stricken or referenced

userStories.title: Renderer Live Layer wired at CLI command boundary
userStories.description: As a maintainer, every CLI command's `render()` helper / direct `console.*` call in `core/src/cli/run.ts` and per-command modules is replaced by `Renderer` service calls. (PRD-B-09 US-157)
acceptanceCriteria: `Renderer` Live Layer instantiated at the command-boundary; commands consume it via `Effect.serviceOption`
`renderer` field added to `GlobalConfig` Schema so the `flag > env > config > default` precedence reaches a real Live Layer (closes the AGENTS interim note)
No `process.stdout.write` or `console.*` calls outside the two §2.4 carve-outs (`core/bin/lando.ts`, `core/src/cli/oclif/pre-renderer.ts`)

userStories.title: §13.4 renderer lint gate
userStories.description: As a maintainer, the lint gate from §13.4 fails CI when any source file outside the two §2.4 carve-outs calls `process.stdout.write` or `console.*`. (PRD-B-09 US-158)
acceptanceCriteria: Lint rule added to `scripts/check-renderer-boundary.ts` (or AST-grep config); CI calls it from `static-checks`
AGENTS.md 'Renderer not wired at the CLI command boundary' interim bullet is removed; §2.4 reverts to its originally-specified prohibitions
Lint gate covers `core/src/**` and `plugins/**`; the two carve-out files are explicitly allowlisted

userStories.title: tooling bootstrap level becomes real
userStories.description: As the runtime, the `tooling` bootstrap level loads what's necessary to dispatch a tooling command — and nothing more. (PRD-B-10 US-159)
acceptanceCriteria: `tooling` `BootstrapLevel` declared per §3.2 ranking; composed Live Layer in `core/src/runtime/layer.ts`
No provider connection / app planner construction at `tooling` level; relies on the §12 app-plan cache

userStories.title: cache-only app-plan read at tooling level
userStories.description: As a tooling command, I read the planned app from the cache; cache miss falls back to a one-shot `app` bootstrap (cold path). (PRD-B-10 US-160)
acceptanceCriteria: Tooling dispatch reads `app-plan` cache by `(landofileContentHash, providerId)`; cache miss → fall back to `app` bootstrap, regenerate plan, repopulate cache
Cache key includes Landofile content hash, included-fragment SHAs, and provider id
Tests cover hit, miss, and stale-entry invalidation

userStories.title: tooling compilation cache
userStories.description: As a tooling command, the compiled command set is cached by Landofile content hash so repeated invocations skip recompilation. (PRD-B-10 US-161)
acceptanceCriteria: Compilation cache stored under §12 cache rules; key = Landofile content hash + tooling-relevant config hash
Cache invalidation on any tooling-affecting key change (services, tooling, includes)
Tests cover cold compile, warm reuse, and invalidation

userStories.title: perf-budget benchmark gate (~150ms target)
userStories.description: As maintainers, a CI benchmark gate measures tooling hot-path latency and fails the run if regression exceeds the budget. (PRD-B-10 US-162)
acceptanceCriteria: Benchmark script (`scripts/bench-tooling-hot-path.ts`) measures `lando <tooling-alias>` cold + warm latency on Linux x64
Budget recorded in a versioned baseline; per-PR regression > 25% fails CI
Target is ~150ms warm-path on Linux x64 per §1.1; current baseline documented even if not yet at target

userStories.title: service-mode lando shell
userStories.description: As a user, I can run `lando shell <service>` to drop into the running container's shell. (PRD-B-10 US-163)
acceptanceCriteria: Service mode is the default for `lando shell <service>`; host mode is `lando shell --host` (Alpha behavior preserved)
Shell is launched via the selected provider's exec, attaches stdin/stdout/stderr, and forwards window-resize signals
AbortSignal forwarding stops the shell on Ctrl-C of the parent CLI

userStories.title: tooling output streams via the renderer task tree
userStories.description: As a user, `lando <tool>` output (whether host or provider-exec) streams into the renderer task tree consistently, matching the §8.9 contract. (PRD-B-10 US-164)
acceptanceCriteria: Tooling commands publish `task.start`/`task.detail`/`task.complete`/`task.fail` events through the renderer
`--renderer=plain` produces a flat stream (preserving the Alpha behavior); `lando` and `verbose` produce the tree

userStories.title: meta:plugin:add against npm (full source)
userStories.description: As a user, I can run `lando meta:plugin:add @lando/plugin-foo` and have it installed under the per-user plugin root with manifest validation. (PRD-B-11 US-165)
acceptanceCriteria: Resolves npm package metadata, downloads tarball, extracts under `<userDataRoot>/plugins/<name>/<version>/`
Manifest validated against `@lando/sdk` plugin manifest schema (`name`, `lando.version`, `requires`)
Re-runs are idempotent; version pinning supported (`@1.2.3` suffix)

userStories.title: meta:plugin:remove
userStories.description: As a user, I can run `lando meta:plugin:remove <name>` and the plugin is unloaded and removed from disk. (PRD-B-11 US-166)
acceptanceCriteria: Plugin directory deleted; per-user plugin registry updated atomically
If the plugin is referenced by an active Landofile, command refuses with a remediation pointing at the referencing app

userStories.title: postinstall trust gating mechanism
userStories.description: As the loader, plugins with declared postinstall scripts are gated behind a trust check; untrusted plugins are installed but inert until trust is granted. (PRD-B-11 US-167)
acceptanceCriteria: `PluginTrustStore` Effect Service tag in `@lando/sdk`; default Live Layer reads `<userConfRoot>/plugin-trust.yml`
`meta:plugin:add` honors trust state; untrusted plugins with postinstalls skip the postinstall and log a remediation pointing at `meta:plugin:trust <name>` (UX finalized at RC)
`meta:plugin:trust <name>` writes the trust entry; `meta:plugin:trust-authoring-root <abs>` allows blanket trust for a developer authoring root

userStories.title: system / user / app plugin discovery sources
userStories.description: As the loader, plugins are discovered from system (bundled), user (`<userDataRoot>/plugins/`), and app (`<appRoot>/.lando/plugins/`) sources, in declared precedence. (PRD-B-11 US-168)
acceptanceCriteria: Discovery returns a merged plugin list; per-source precedence is `app > user > system`
Conflict on the same plugin id across sources resolves to the highest-precedence entry; warning emitted
Tests cover all three sources individually + combined

userStories.title: plugin command-index cache
userStories.description: As the CLI, repeated `lando --help` after a plugin install hits the §12 plugin-command-index cache and does not re-scan plugin directories. (PRD-B-11 US-169)
acceptanceCriteria: Cache encodes plugin-list SHA + per-plugin command id list
Tests cover cold scan, warm hit, and post-add invalidation

userStories.title: @lando/core/* entry points published
userStories.description: As a library embedder, I can import from `@lando/core/schema`, `/errors`, `/events`, `/services`, `/testing`, `/cli`, and `/oclif` and get the expected surface. (PRD-B-11 US-170)
acceptanceCriteria: `package.json#exports` lists every entry point with TS types + ESM
Each entry is documented in `core/README.md` or a dedicated `docs/embedding.md` per §16
Tests in `core/test/library/` cover at least one symbol from each entry

userStories.title: EmbeddingPluginPolicy fully wired
userStories.description: As an embedder, I can pass an `EmbeddingPluginPolicy` to `makeLandoRuntime` and control plugin discovery / loading per §16. (PRD-B-11 US-171)
acceptanceCriteria: `makeLandoRuntime({ plugins: { policy: … } })` honors the policy; `none` mode loads no plugins at all
Library mode defaults: silent logger, json renderer, `policy: "explicit"`, no telemetry

userStories.title: import-boundary test enforces OCLIF-free default entry
userStories.description: As a maintainer, the default `@lando/core` entry imports no OCLIF code path — OCLIF lives only under `@lando/core/oclif`. (PRD-B-11 US-172)
acceptanceCriteria: Import-boundary test in `core/test/library/` resolves every symbol from `@lando/core` and asserts no OCLIF module is loaded transitively
Test also covers the four critical entry points (`/cli`, `/oclif`, `/testing`, default) and asserts their bundled module graph at compile-time
Failure messages name the offending import chain

userStories.title: library-mode defaults
userStories.description: As an embedder, `makeLandoRuntime` in library mode uses silent logger, json renderer, no plugin auto-discovery, and no telemetry — unless I opt in. (PRD-B-11 US-173)
acceptanceCriteria: Defaults applied at runtime construction; embedders can override per-field
Default-renderer choice in library mode does not pull TUI code paths (verified by the import-boundary test)
Tests cover the default-set and the explicit-override path

userStories.title: plugin SDK contract test publishable
userStories.description: As a plugin author, `@lando/sdk/test` publishes a runnable contract suite I can wire into my plugin's test script. (PRD-B-11 US-174)
acceptanceCriteria: `@lando/sdk/test` exports the plugin-SDK contract suite (covering manifest, plugin layer, contribution surfaces, error contract)
At least one external example plugin in `plugins/` (or a fixture under `core/test/library/`) imports + runs the suite green

userStories.title: <Inspect> component (read-only state assertions inside a step)
userStories.description: As a guide author, I can place `<Inspect>` between `<Run>` and `<Verify>` to expose intermediate state (config, env, file contents) without affecting scenario behavior. (PRD-B-12 US-175)
acceptanceCriteria: Generator emits `ctx.inspect(…)` calls; output is captured in the transcript but not asserted
Tests cover schema validation and a sample guide using `<Inspect>` against a fake state

userStories.title: replace ScenarioContext.runCli fake with real CLI seam
userStories.description: As the scenario engine, `ctx.runCli(args)` invokes the real Lando CLI via `@lando/core/cli` (or compiled binary in e2e mode) instead of Alpha2's fake. (PRD-B-12 US-176)
acceptanceCriteria: `runCli` uses `@lando/core/cli` for `layer: "scenario"`; the e2e layer uses the compiled binary
Fake path remains available behind `ScenarioContextFactory.testOnlyFake` for unit tests within the engine itself
Existing Alpha2 generated tests still pass without changes

userStories.title: <Tabs> + <Tab> for axis selection
userStories.description: As a guide author, I can declare `<Tabs>` with multiple `<Tab>` children to express axis selection (e.g. service framework, OS, language version) and the renderer shows tabs while the codegen produces a scenario per tab. (PRD-B-12 US-177)
acceptanceCriteria: `tabs:` frontmatter declares axis names; per-tab content emits its own scenario variant
Lint catches missing axis declarations and duplicate tab ids

userStories.title: multi-axis variants (axes: frontmatter + per-cell variants:)
userStories.description: As a guide author, I can declare multiple `axes:` and have the codegen emit one scenario per cartesian-product cell, optionally overriding per-cell `variants:`. (PRD-B-12 US-178)
acceptanceCriteria: `axes:` frontmatter accepts an array of axis declarations; codegen produces scenario variants accordingly
Per-cell `variants:` overrides apply through a documented merge order
Generated test files carry `// @variant: <key>=<value>` headers (replaces the empty placeholder from Alpha2 progress)

userStories.title: <Hidden> becomes code-emitting
userStories.description: As a guide author, `<Hidden>` blocks emit code into the generated scenario but are not rendered in the reader-visible transcript. (PRD-B-12 US-179)
acceptanceCriteria: Codegen treats `<Hidden>` as a non-rendered emitter; Alpha2's `NotImplementedError` remediation is removed
Lint requires a `reason` attribute on `<Hidden>` (≥ 8 chars) for maintainability
Tests cover round-trip through schema and a representative use (e.g. seeding test fixtures invisibly)

userStories.title: <Inline> + <Skip> components
userStories.description: As a guide author, I can use `<Inline>` for inline-rendered code samples that do not execute, and `<Skip>` to mark a scenario as skipped with a reason. (PRD-B-12 US-180)
acceptanceCriteria: `<Inline>` does not emit any execution code; output appears verbatim in transcripts
`<Skip>` requires a `reason` (≥ 8 chars); generated test calls `test.skip(...)`

userStories.title: fuller §19.10 lint depth
userStories.description: As CI, `bun run lint:guides` runs the full §19.10 lint set: matcher-shape validation, fixture-immutability checks, axis-id uniqueness, reason-required gates, and unused-fixture detection. (PRD-B-12 US-181)
acceptanceCriteria: Every §19.10 lint rule documented in `scripts/lint-guides.ts` (or equivalent)
Each rule has at least one failing fixture and one passing fixture under `test/fixtures/guides/lint/`
Lint gate from PRD-A2-04 extends to the new rules in Beta

userStories.title: recipe README strip/flatten (§19.13)
userStories.description: As a recipe author, my canonical recipe's MDX README can be authored with executable-guide components, and the build pipeline strips them into plain README for npm publishing while keeping the original for the docs site. (PRD-B-12 US-182)
acceptanceCriteria: `scripts/build-recipe-readmes.ts` (Alpha2 stub) is now active; emits per-recipe `README.md` (stripped, plain Markdown) from the MDX source
At least one canonical Alpha recipe ships an MDX-authored README that passes through the pipeline
Generated stripped READMEs do not contain executable-component artifacts and validate as plain Markdown

userStories.title: build darwin-arm64 binary
userStories.description: As CI, the `build-darwin-arm64` job produces `dist/lando-darwin-arm64` via `bun build --compile` and runs smoke + `--help` checks before uploading the artifact. (PRD-B-13 US-183)
acceptanceCriteria: Runner is `macos-14` (or current Apple-silicon image); Bun version pinned per `.bun-version`

userStories.title: build darwin-x64 binary
userStories.description: As CI, the `build-darwin-x64` job mirrors US-183 for Intel macOS. (PRD-B-13 US-184)
acceptanceCriteria: Runner is `macos-13` (or current Intel image); same smoke as US-183

userStories.title: build linux-arm64 binary
userStories.description: As CI, the `build-linux-arm64` job produces `dist/lando-linux-arm64` and runs the same smoke + `--help` checks. (PRD-B-13 US-185)
acceptanceCriteria: Runner uses `ubuntu-24.04-arm64` (or QEMU emulation if hosted ARM64 unavailable); Bun version pinned

userStories.title: build windows-x64 binary
userStories.description: As CI, the `build-windows-x64` job produces `dist/lando-windows-x64.exe` and runs the same smoke + `--help` checks. (PRD-B-13 US-186)
acceptanceCriteria: Runner is `windows-2022`; Bun version pinned via `.bun-version`
Smoke covers exit codes + UTF-8 stdout decoding

userStories.title: AOT bootstrap-layer codegen (§17.2)
userStories.description: As a maintainer, the runtime no longer uses runtime `Layer.mergeAll`/`Layer.provide` chains outside the §17.2 codegen output; bootstrap layers are emitted ahead of time. (PRD-B-13 US-187)
acceptanceCriteria: Codegen script (`scripts/build-bootstrap-layers.ts`) emits per-bootstrap-level Live Layers under `core/src/runtime/generated/`
`core/src/runtime/layer.ts` consumes the generated module; no runtime `Layer.mergeAll` outside the codegen output
AGENTS.md 'AOT bootstrap-layer codegen not yet shipped' interim bullet removed

userStories.title: asset embedding for recipes / schemas / OCLIF manifest
userStories.description: As the compiled binary, bundled recipes, schemas, and the OCLIF manifest are embedded at build time — no runtime FS read of these assets. (PRD-B-13 US-188)
acceptanceCriteria: Build pipeline reads recipes / schemas / OCLIF manifest at compile time and emits them into the binary's data section
Import-boundary test asserts no runtime FS read of `recipes/`, `sdk/schema/`, or `oclif.manifest.json` from the compiled binary path
`bundled.ts` is populated by codegen (`scripts/build-bundled-plugins.ts`); hand-edits forbidden

userStories.title: per-PR CI matrix on all 5 platforms
userStories.description: As CI, every PR runs `static-checks`, `build-<platform>`, and `provider-integration-<platform>` (where supported) on all five platforms. (PRD-B-13 US-189)
acceptanceCriteria: All §13.1 test layers (unit, Effect service, CLI, scenario, recipe, library, provider contract) run green on every platform
Compile time + test wall-clock are tracked; per-platform timeout caps documented

userStories.title: nightly e2e against @lando/provider-lando
userStories.description: As CI, a nightly cron job runs the full e2e scenario suite against `@lando/provider-lando` on Linux x64. (PRD-B-13 US-190)
acceptanceCriteria: `.github/workflows/nightly.yml` runs the `@smoke` + non-smoke e2e tags against a real provider-lando setup
Failures notify maintainers (Slack/Issue mechanism out of scope here — Beta only ships the workflow)

userStories.title: distribution rehearsal in nightly job
userStories.description: As CI, the nightly job rehearses the full distribution flow (build, package, upload artifact set) without actually publishing to GitHub Releases. (PRD-B-13 US-191)
acceptanceCriteria: Rehearsal produces a tagged artifact bundle (binaries + sha256 sums) under workflow-artifact storage
Acceptance check verifies SHA256 sums match the binaries

userStories.title: weekly provider matrix
userStories.description: As CI, a weekly cron job runs the provider contract suite against Docker Desktop, Docker Engine, Podman Desktop, Podman, Lima, and OrbStack on the platforms each supports. (PRD-B-13 US-192)
acceptanceCriteria: `.github/workflows/provider-matrix.yml` declares the weekly cron; matrix cells skip where the engine is not installable in CI
Cells run the `@lando/sdk/test` provider contract suite (US-080)

userStories.title: publish @lando/core 4.0.0-beta.N on the next npm tag
userStories.description: As a publisher, the release pipeline tags `4.0.0-beta.N` and publishes `@lando/core` (and the bundled plugin packages) on the npm `next` tag. (PRD-B-13 US-193)
acceptanceCriteria: `scripts/release.ts` handles tag → publish for the workspace packages (no signing yet — that's RC)
`next` tag points at the latest beta after each publish; `dev` tag is retired at Beta cutover via `npm dist-tag rm` on `@lando/core` and bundled plugin packages

userStories.title: plugin SDK contract tests published
userStories.description: As a plugin author, the Beta SDK contract suite (`@lando/sdk/test`) is published with the npm release and runnable from a downstream plugin's test command. (PRD-B-13 US-194)
acceptanceCriteria: `@lando/sdk/test` is in `package.json#exports`; the contract suite runner has documented args
At least one bundled plugin (`@lando/provider-lando` or `@lando/service-lando`) runs the contract suite in its package tests
